presentation.title: The hoist is drifting
presentation.body: A loaded harbour hoist drifts downward. Before opening the cabinet, recall what equal 200 N upward and downward forces would mean for its resultant.

screen.front: A hoist has 200 N upward and 200 N downward force. What is its resultant force?
screen.back: The resultant is 0 N. Equal magnitude forces in opposite directions cancel.

feedback.success: You committed to a recall attempt before seeing the model answer. Use your self grade to choose the right amount of support.
hints: Keep both magnitude and direction when you add forces.

accessibility.ariaLabel: Recall the resultant force on the hoist
accessibility.textAlternative: The card states both force magnitudes and directions in text.
accessibility.reducedMotionAlternative: The recall card has no timed motion.

presentation.body: Take upward as positive. The two equal readings are $+200\,\text{N} + (-200\,\text{N}) = 0\,\text{N}$. The hoist can be balanced even while it has forces acting on it.

screen.body: The sign records direction. Adding a downward force means adding a negative value when upward is positive.
screen.selfExplainPrompt: Why is the downward 200 N written as a negative value in this sum?
screen.selfExplainAnswer: The negative sign records that the downward force points opposite to the chosen positive direction. It is not a smaller force.

feedback.success: You explained why direction belongs in the sum. Now use that rule on the real readings.

accessibility.textAlternative: The signed force calculation is written in the scene text.
accessibility.reducedMotionAlternative: The explanation is shown as text without animation.

presentation.body: The engineer gives you two controlled force readings. Change only the upward support reading, record both observations, then infer the rule before choosing a new setting.

props.title: Hoist force investigation
props.predictionPrompt: With a 200 N downward load, what will a 160 N upward support produce?

trials.observationLabel: With 160 N upward and 200 N downward, the resultant is 40 N downward.
With 200 N upward and 200 N downward, the resultant is 0 N.

props.inferencePrompt: Which rule accounts for both readings?

rules.label: The resultant is the signed sum of forces, so equal opposing forces cancel.
Add force magnitudes without direction to find the resultant.
Only the largest force determines the resultant.

props.transferPrompt: For 120 N upward and 90 N downward, what should the resultant be?

feedback.success: The two observations and the transfer case agree: resultant force is a signed sum, not an unsigned total.
feedback.failure: Run both readings, choose the rule that matches both, then apply it to the new force pair.
misconceptions: The unsigned total 360 N ignores direction, so it cannot explain opposing forces cancelling.
The largest force is not enough information because the other force changes the signed sum.

accessibility.ariaLabel: Investigate force readings on the hoist
accessibility.textAlternative: Every force reading and observation is available as text on a labelled button.
accessibility.reducedMotionAlternative: Observations appear as text without a timed simulation.

presentation.speaker: Harbour engineer
presentation.body: The load drifts downward when released. Use the reading as evidence. Do not tighten a part until your diagnosis explains the direction of the imbalance.

diagnoses.label: The upward support force is too small
The brake is jammed
diagnoses.feedback: A jammed brake would oppose motion. The measured 160 N upward support is smaller than the 200 N downward load, which directly explains the downward resultant.

repairs.label: Increase the upward support to 200 N

feedback.success: You used the symptom and force readings to select the repair. Restoring 200 N upward support gives a zero resultant against the 200 N downward load.
feedback.failure: Observe the symptom, commit the force reading, then choose a diagnosis and repair that fit both.
misconceptions: A repair should follow the measured imbalance, not a plausible sounding fault.
hints: The force readings are required evidence.
Compare the direction of the 40 N resultant with the direction of the drift.

accessibility.ariaLabel: Diagnose and repair the harbour hoist
accessibility.textAlternative: The symptom, evidence, diagnoses, and repairs are exposed as labelled native controls.
accessibility.reducedMotionAlternative: The repair consequence is described in text without animation.

presentation.body: The hoist is steady. You first recalled the equal force case, then used two readings to infer a signed-sum rule, and finally chose the repair that restores a zero resultant.

termination.summary: You can calculate a resultant force and use it as evidence for a causal repair. Return for the standalone review after a delay.

accessibility.textAlternative: The completed repair and the learning payoff are stated in text.
accessibility.reducedMotionAlternative: Completion has a text alternative when reduced motion is preferred.
